title: Basic Story

jokes.text: The hell was that? - What was what? Long, quiet lull. Don't like it. It's called contentment.
jokes.setup: Character notices an unusually peaceful moment in their chaotic lives
jokes.punchline: Another character identifies the unfamiliar feeling as 'contentment'
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about the show's own formula - characters are suspicious of peace because chaos is their norm. Strong character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You know, it's weird. Everything's actually going okay right now. Nobody's fighting, nobody's failing a class, there's no elaborate scheme blowing up in our faces.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I know what you mean. It's strange.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's contentment.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Contentment? Is that what this is? I forgot what it felt like.

jokes.text: I commented on his commentary. I just drew a circle around Abed, baby.
jokes.setup: Jeff realizes he's commenting on Abed's meta-commentary
jokes.punchline: Proudly declares he's out-meta'd the meta character
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that could only work on Community. Jeff's smug satisfaction at beating Abed at his own game is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, I just realized I'm commenting on your meta-commentary about me commenting on your meta-commentary.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm out-meta'd the meta character. I'm the meta now.

jokes.text: I don't think it's nice to pigeonhole people's gimmicks.
jokes.setup: Discussion about characters being self-aware of their roles
jokes.punchline: Abed protests pigeonholing while acknowledging everyone has 'gimmicks'
jokes.explanation: Classic Abed - self-aware about being self-aware, delivered with characteristic deadpan sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm not a gimmick. I'm a person who happens to reference pop culture and watch a lot of TV.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Abed, you literally just described a gimmick.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Fine. But so does everyone else here. Britta's the neurotic one, Troy's the innocent one, Annie's the overachiever, Shirley's the mom, Pierce is the old guy—

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Hey!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: —and Jeff, you're the handsome one who coasts on his looks.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's not a gimmick, that's just who I am.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: You hear me, Two-Voice?
jokes.setup: Abed addressing someone with a distinctive vocal trait
jokes.punchline: Reduces character to their most obvious characteristic
jokes.explanation: Minor character observation joke, economical but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Hey, nice voice.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Thanks!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's like you're a person, except you're just a voice.

jokes.text: Attention, students and faculty of Greendale. I pressed this button on accident... but might as well check in. How are you?
jokes.setup: Professional PA announcement from authority figure
jokes.punchline: Admits it was accidental but decides to have a casual chat anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dean moment - awkward oversharing and treating students like personal friends.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Attention students, this is Dean Pelton. I accidentally hit the PA button, but since I have your attention, let me tell you about some exciting campus initiatives.

jokes.text: The information you've requested... Is on the Internet.
jokes.setup: Student asking for information assistance
jokes.punchline: Unhelpful but technically accurate response delivered with authority
jokes.explanation: Classic unhelpful bureaucratic response, perfectly delivered by Leonard's character.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Can you help me find information about this topic?

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Well, information exists, and this topic is definitely a thing that people talk about. You're welcome.

jokes.text: Your ass is getting appraised, insurance-style.
jokes.setup: Explaining insurance appraisal process
jokes.punchline: Sexualizes mundane bureaucratic procedure
jokes.explanation: Cheap sexual innuendo that doesn't really land. Relies on 'random = funny' rather than wit.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: The insurance appraisal process involves a thorough examination of the property. We need to inspect every inch, run our hands along the surfaces, check all the tight spots, and make sure everything is properly... positioned.

jokes.text: Are you guys drunk? - Good luck proving it before we're not.
jokes.setup: Jeff notices suspicious behavior and asks direct question
jokes.punchline: Admission of guilt with confident legal loophole
jokes.explanation: Clever reversal - instead of denying, they acknowledge and dare him to prove it. Good structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, did you just admit to that?

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: Yes, but my lawyer says I can't be prosecuted because technically I was acting in my capacity as a school board member at the time.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's not how the law works.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: Isn't it though?

jokes.text: Pa-pa-papaya. Mango. Strawberry. - Too ripe.
jokes.setup: Characters intensely focused on fruit matching game
jokes.punchline: Britta critiques virtual fruit ripeness with genuine concern
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor about taking trivial things seriously. Britta's earnestness sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. This banana isn't ripe enough. Look at the color — it's still too green. These virtual bananas need to be a deeper yellow.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Britta, it's a video game.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I don't care. Standards are standards.

jokes.text: It's 3 dollars. What are you playing? That's control software for some kind of irrigation equipment.
jokes.setup: Troy claims to have spent $199 on entertainment
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's actually $3 irrigation software they're playing like a game
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up expectation of expensive game, reveals mundane reality. Perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I spent $199 on entertainment.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: What did you buy?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: It's $3 irrigation software. We're playing it like a game.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You spent $199 on $3 software?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: In-app purchases.

jokes.text: Call to adventure. Call to adventure.
jokes.setup: Dean announces potential crisis to the group
jokes.punchline: Abed immediately identifies it as a story structure beat
jokes.explanation: Pure Abed - instantly recognizes their lives as following narrative structure. Meta-commentary at its finest.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We have a crisis on our hands.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's the inciting incident.

jokes.text: Something that sounds normal and boring but is scary. Like a raised mole or a turkey deep fryer.
jokes.setup: Asked to explain what makes insurance appraisers scary
jokes.punchline: Compares to oddly specific mundane-but-threatening things
jokes.explanation: Clever observation about things that are simultaneously mundane and ominous. The specificity makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: They're like if a DMV employee and a hitman had a baby. They show up at your door with a clipboard and a smile that says 'I've seen the receipts, and I know what you did.'

jokes.text: Only one man qualified to appraise insurance... and he died 2000 years ago on a cross.
jokes.setup: Discussion of insurance appraisal expertise
jokes.punchline: Implies Jesus Christ was an insurance appraiser
jokes.explanation: Classic Pierce - inappropriate religious reference that's absurd enough to be funny rather than offensive.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Jesus Christ was an insurance appraiser.

jokes.text: I've seen insurance appraisers bleed. Their blood's different, darker.
jokes.setup: Pierce building mystique around insurance appraisers
jokes.punchline: Describes them as literally inhuman with different blood
jokes.explanation: Escalates the absurd mythology Pierce is building. Darkly comic and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Insurance appraisers? They're not like us. They don't have blood like we do. Their blood is different. Colder. They can sense fear.

jokes.text: You're like Hurricane Katrina dogs that won't let anyone pet them anymore.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining why the group overreacts to potential problems
jokes.punchline: Compares them to traumatized disaster animals
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor that's both funny and psychologically accurate. Dark but insightful character observation.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You guys are like traumatized disaster animals. A fire alarm goes off and you all scatter like you're fleeing a tsunami.

jokes.text: I wrote a paper on those dogs.
jokes.setup: Jeff's Hurricane Katrina dogs metaphor
jokes.punchline: Annie matter-of-factly reveals she's literally studied this exact topic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Annie moment - her academic overachievement makes Jeff's metaphor accidentally literal. Great callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Look, it's like Hurricane Katrina dogs. They're traumatized, they need time to adjust, they might bite you.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Actually, I've studied post-traumatic stress disorder in canines. The recovery period is typically 18 to 24 months, and aggression is only present in about 12% of cases.

jokes.text: There is no story. - Everything's a story, Jeff.
jokes.setup: Jeff insists their situation isn't following narrative structure
jokes.punchline: Abed counters with meta-textual certainty that everything is story
jokes.explanation: Central conflict of the episode encapsulated. Jeff vs. Abed represents normal vs. meta perspectives on narrative.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: This isn't a story. We're not following some narrative structure.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Jeff, everything is a story. We're always in a story. Even this conversation is a story about a conversation in a story.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's not helpful.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: No, but it's true. And the fact that you're frustrated proves you know it's true, because in a story, the character always resists the truth right before they accept it.

jokes.text: Is this a wedding ring? - Are you kidding? What...?
jokes.setup: Hickey finds a ring and asks if it's for marriage
jokes.punchline: Jeff's shocked reaction implies he's not ready for that assumption
jokes.explanation: Minor character beat playing on Jeff's commitment issues. Serviceable but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Is this a ring? Are you proposing to somebody?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What? No! I'm not... that's not... why would you think that?

jokes.text: It's my mom's. I started wearing it because... I don't know, everybody dreams of settling down, right? And because it fits. She had huge fingers for a woman. Part of what killed her, really.
jokes.setup: Sentimental explanation about wearing his mother's ring
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that her large fingers contributed to her death
jokes.explanation: Classic Hickey - starts sweet and emotional, ends with casual dark revelation. Perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: I wear this ring because it was my mother's. She had large fingers, and that's what killed her.

jokes.text: This is going to be the most boring thing to happen here... since Britta dated Troy.
jokes.setup: Jeff predicting the appraisal will be uneventful
jokes.punchline: References off-screen relationship as epitome of boring
jokes.explanation: Good character burn that references past show events. Jeff's casual cruelty toward absent friends.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: This appraisal is going to be completely uneventful.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: It'll be like watching Britta and Troy date.

jokes.text: Wait. If there's no story, what am I explaining? I'm in a story.
jokes.setup: Abed trying to accept Jeff's 'no story' premise
jokes.punchline: Realizes the act of explaining proves he's in a story
jokes.explanation: Meta-breakthrough moment. Abed's self-awareness creates paradox - he can't escape being in a story.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Wait, if there's no story, then why am I explaining that there's no story? The fact that I'm explaining it means I'm in a story explaining that there's no story, which means there IS a story.

jokes.text: Hi, my name is Ronald Mohammed. Go ahead, heard them all.
jokes.setup: Professional introduction from insurance appraiser
jokes.punchline: Preemptively acknowledges his name will generate jokes/comments
jokes.explanation: Minor character beat that establishes the appraiser as weary of name-based humor. Functional setup.

dialogue.speaker: Ronald Mohammed
dialogue.line: Yes, I know what my name is. Yes, it's real. No, I don't want to hear your jokes about it. And yes, before you ask, my parents hated me.

jokes.text: But the city defines a dog... as any living entity with four legs and a tail. So raccoons, bears... mountain lions, mice, these are all just different sizes of dog.
jokes.setup: Bureaucratic explanation of liability definitions
jokes.punchline: Reveals absurdly broad definition that makes all mammals 'dogs'
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist bureaucracy humor. Escalating list of increasingly dangerous 'dogs' is perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Ronald Mohammed
dialogue.line: According to section 4, subsection B of the university liability code, a 'dog' is defined as any mammal weighing over 2 pounds that has been on campus for more than 24 consecutive hours.

dialogue.speaker: Ronald Mohammed
dialogue.line: Which technically means... you're all dogs.

jokes.text: You got something in your teeth. - You got something in your chest. Four hundred thousand gallons of liquefied horse meat.
jokes.setup: Dean points out Jeff has something in his teeth
jokes.punchline: Jeff escalates to accusing Dean of having liquidated horse meat in his chest
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation that's darkly funny but feels slightly random. Jeff's stress manifesting in weird aggression.

dialogue.speaker: Dean
dialogue.line: Jeff, you have something in your teeth.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well Dean, you have liquidated horse meat in your chest.

jokes.text: And you shall find that salt is the taste of another man's bread... and hard is the way up and down another man's stairs. Dante.
jokes.setup: Professional insurance appraiser in bureaucratic setting
jokes.punchline: Randomly quotes classical literature with pretentious attribution
jokes.explanation: Odd character beat that makes the appraiser more mysterious/pretentious. Somewhat overwrought but fits the absurd tone.

dialogue.speaker: Ronald Mohammed
dialogue.line: As Camus once said, 'The only way to deal with an unfree world is to become so absolutely free that very existence is an act of rebellion.'

dialogue.speaker: Ronald Mohammed
dialogue.line: Or was that Sartre? No matter. The point is, your claim is denied.

jokes.text: Me with a beard? Keep fighting the story. Keep fighting it until it falls apart. I'm right. I mean, I'm wrong. I mean, I'm right. I mean, I'm wrong.
jokes.setup: Abed trying to break free from narrative structure
jokes.punchline: His attempt to be non-narrative becomes increasingly frantic and contradictory
jokes.explanation: Abed's breakdown as he realizes he can't escape story structure. Meta-comedy becoming psychological comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm going to break free from narrative structure. No more three-act format, no character arcs, no thematic resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm going to be completely unpredictable and random!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But first, let me explain my motivation in a lengthy monologue that perfectly encapsulates my character journey.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: No wait, I'm not doing that. I'm doing the opposite. Everything backwards. Backwards!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I will now speak only in non-sequiturs and absurdist humor to defy conventional comedy structure, which is a clearly established character trait that shows consistent character development.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Why am I still talking about this? I'm overthinking it, which is ironic because I said I wouldn't think about structure, but thinking about not thinking about structure is itself a structure, so—

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You know what? Forget it. I'm free now.

jokes.text: Unsubscribe.
jokes.setup: Abed's continued struggle with narrative imprisonment
jokes.punchline: Treats reality like an unwanted email subscription
jokes.explanation: Perfect single-word punchline. Abed applying digital logic to existential crisis is pure character gold.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I treat reality like an unwanted email subscription. I don't read it, I just delete it and mark it as spam.

jokes.text: For the final stage of my appraisal... I'm gonna randomly select one of your vending machines... to see if it can be rocked using human strength... enough to tip and crush me.
jokes.setup: Professional explaining final assessment procedure
jokes.punchline: Casually describes testing if vending machine can kill him
jokes.explanation: Darkly absurd bureaucratic procedure. The casual tone makes the death-testing funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: So for your final assessment, we're going to need you to demonstrate your understanding of campus safety protocols.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We'll be testing various scenarios, including the vending machine on the third floor. You know, just to see if it can kill you.

jokes.text: Hey, look. It's Ronald Mohammed. How about that name?
jokes.setup: Board member notices the appraiser
jokes.punchline: Makes exactly the kind of comment about his name that he said he'd heard before
jokes.explanation: Decent callback that fulfills the earlier setup. Shows the board member's insensitivity.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: Wait, your name is Dick Appraiser?

dialogue.speaker: Dick Appraiser
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: That's got to be the worst name I've ever heard.

jokes.text: Maybe we're not as smart as you. I mean, we only went to Yale.
jokes.setup: Board member feigning intellectual inferiority
jokes.punchline: Humble-brags about Ivy League education while claiming stupidity
jokes.explanation: Standard elite sarcasm. Competent character work but predictable structure.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: I'm not the smartest person in the room, I'll admit that. I mean, I only went to Harvard.

jokes.text: We should call T.J.Maxx right away. - Dude, I told you. This jacket looks fine. - No, to talk about selling this place.
jokes.setup: Board member suggests calling T.J.Maxx for business
jokes.punchline: Other member thinks he's criticizing his clothing before clarification
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection that reveals insecurity about fashion. Good character detail in throwaway moment.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: We should call T.J.Maxx for business advice.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 2
dialogue.line: Hey, there's nothing wrong with my clothes!

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: What? No, the store T.J.Maxx — for business strategies.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 2
dialogue.line: Oh... I thought you were making fun of my outfit.

jokes.text: Oh, Richie, you're my friend, but that is the last thing you need. - Tough love received, bro.
jokes.setup: Discussion of calling Ben & Jerry's for business
jokes.punchline: Board member thinks partner is fat-shaming him about ice cream
jokes.explanation: Another misdirection about business vs. personal concerns. Shows the board members' dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: We should call Ben & Jerry's about a business partnership.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 2
dialogue.line: That's a great idea! They could sponsor our events.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'll reach out to them personally.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 2
dialogue.line: Maybe skip the phone call and just send them an email... from your home address.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: Why would I do that?

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 2
dialogue.line: No reason. Just seems like the professional approach.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: Are you saying I shouldn't call them because of how I look?!

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 2
dialogue.line: What? No! I just—

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member 1
dialogue.line: You think Ben & Jerry are going to judge me for my weight!

jokes.text: Let me be one of the six this year.
jokes.setup: Dean's despair over Greendale being sold
jokes.punchline: References earlier statistic about vending machine deaths, wants to be a casualty
jokes.explanation: Dark callback that shows Dean's genuine despair. Perfectly structured callback with emotional weight.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: More people die from vending machine accidents than from actual accidents. So if Greendale is sold, I'm going to throw myself at a vending machine until it falls on me.

jokes.text: If I come over there... there are gonna be two sounds: me hitting you twice.
jokes.setup: Jeff threatening someone off-screen
jokes.punchline: Very specific and matter-of-fact description of violence
jokes.explanation: Great Jeff threat with perfect economy. The mathematical precision of 'two sounds: me hitting you twice' is wonderfully absurd.

jokes.text: Okay, okay, I should've written this out. I thought I'd be able to come up with something on the fly.
jokes.setup: Dean struggling with impromptu farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Admits his failure mid-speech to the entire school
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy - public failure made worse by acknowledging it publicly. Very Dean-like.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I came here today to say goodbye, to reflect on my time at Greendale, to inspire you all with words of wisdom and—

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: You know what? I have no idea what I'm doing. I never have. I'm just a man in a suit making it up as I go along, hoping no one notices.

jokes.text: The reason I have 48 followers is that I don't exploit them. They come to me for atheistic rants and photos of my meals... not this obnoxiousness.
jokes.setup: Britta explaining why she won't promote the save campaign
jokes.punchline: Describes her Twitter content as atheistic rants and meal photos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Britta - takes her tiny following seriously while admitting she posts typical boring content. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I can't promote the save campaign. My Twitter is just atheistic rants and meal photos.

jokes.text: Just ask Chris Brown or China.
jokes.setup: Annie explaining how internet attention changes things
jokes.punchline: Compares domestic abuser to entire nation as examples of internet consequences
jokes.explanation: Odd pairing that's economical but feels slightly random. Annie's naive conflation of different internet phenomena.

jokes.text: Treasure. Buried treasure.
jokes.setup: Group desperately seeking solutions to save Greendale
jokes.punchline: Pierce's contribution is simple-minded treasure hunting
jokes.explanation: Typical Pierce solution - childishly simple and irrelevant. In character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: What if there's treasure buried somewhere on campus? We could dig it up and sell it!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, that's... that's not how anything works.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're saying you've never found treasure before? Well, I have. Twice. Three times if you count that money I found in my couch.

jokes.text: Screw you, guys. It's time to get paid.
jokes.setup: Chang's seemingly helpful question about saving Greendale
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been working for Subway all along
jokes.explanation: Typical Chang betrayal that's both predictable and satisfying. Good character consistency even if not surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about ways to save Greendale. What if we partnered with a major corporation? A restaurant chain, perhaps?

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: I mean, I've been working with them for months now. Subway's really committed to supporting educational institutions.

jokes.text: Chang just unexpectedly betrayed the group for the last time.
jokes.setup: Chang's betrayal moment
jokes.punchline: Abed notes it's 'unexpected' while clearly being entirely expected
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed meta-commentary. Ironically calls it 'unexpected' while acknowledging it's Chang's pattern. Great writing.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That was unexpected.

jokes.text: You just said you were proud of it. - Proud of its potential. Love its potential.
jokes.setup: Board member claiming pride in Greendale
jokes.punchline: Clarifies they're only proud of its potential, not its reality
jokes.explanation: Standard corporate double-speak. Competent character work showing board's hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: I'm very proud of Greendale Community College.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: Well, proud of its potential. The reality is a dumpster fire, but the potential? Chef's kiss.

jokes.text: Subway doesn't call its bread 'buns,' and we don't serve white. We serve Italian, honey oat... nine-grain wheat. - Thanks for making my joke accurate. Now it's hilarious.
jokes.setup: Subway rep correcting Britta's terminology
jokes.punchline: Britta sarcastically thanks him for ruining her joke with accuracy
jokes.explanation: Great meta-joke about how corporate corrections kill comedy. Britta's sarcasm is perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: It's actually a footlong.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Thanks for ruining my joke with accuracy.

jokes.text: Wait. You don't call your bread 'buns.' What do you call them? - Bread. - Bread is a substance. What do you call the units of bread you use, 'breads'?
jokes.setup: Annie pursuing logical inconsistency in Subway terminology
jokes.punchline: Traps rep in linguistic paradox about bread units
jokes.explanation: Perfect Annie - uses academic precision to dismantle corporate speak. Good escalation of earlier bread joke.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: So if a footlong is twelve inches, why do you call it a footlong? A foot is twelve inches. That's the definition of a foot.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: It's just what we call it.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: But that's circular reasoning. The name describes the measurement, but the measurement doesn't match the name. So either the footlong isn't a foot long, or a foot isn't twelve inches, which means every ruler in the world is wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: Look, I just make sandwiches.

jokes.text: I got one of these label things. I mean, it makes custom labels. This was gonna help me get organised.
jokes.setup: Dean showing his organizational efforts
jokes.punchline: Pathetic hope placed in a simple label maker
jokes.explanation: Sad Dean moment that perfectly captures his misguided optimism. The label maker as solution to all problems is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I've organized all the files by color, cross-referenced them by department, and labeled everything with my new label maker.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: It's pathetic hope placed in a simple label maker.

jokes.text: And that's Russell Borchert. - Borchert, Borchert, loved computers... Yes, yes, 'More than women's butts or hooters.' Very mature, you two.
jokes.setup: Dean mentions historical Greendale figure Russell Borchert
jokes.punchline: Jeff and Annie immediately recite juvenile rhyme about him
jokes.explanation: Shows how Greendale legends become crude nursery rhymes. Dean's exasperated response is good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Russell Borchert, Russell Borchert, smells like a burned shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Russell Borchert, Russell Borchert, keeps his toys in a locked drawer.

jokes.text: By the way, he was a millionaire and a genius. He has sex with one computer, and that's his legacy?
jokes.setup: Dean explaining Borchert's actual accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Laments that sexual legend overshadows real achievements
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that shows Dean's genuine frustration with how history gets distorted. Nice character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: You know, it's a shame really. Borchert's actual accomplishments - his groundbreaking work in administration, his innovative approaches to institutional management - all completely overshadowed by this ridiculous sexual legend.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Nobody remembers the real achievements. It's always just the scandals, the rumors, the... the sexual exploits.

jokes.text: Think you could make the transition to teaching Sandwich Law? - It's essentially the same, I'm sure, just a bit more focused.
jokes.setup: Subway offering Jeff a teaching position
jokes.punchline: Jeff treats 'Sandwich Law' as legitimate academic specialization
jokes.explanation: Jeff's willingness to rationalize anything for job security. Absurd concept treated with professional seriousness.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: We'd like to offer you a teaching position at Subway University.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Sandwich Law? That's a legitimate academic specialization.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: It's more of a corporate training program, but sure.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm going to need tenure and a corner office that overlooks the condiment station.

jokes.text: That's a Subway black card. It entitles you to 5-dollar footlongs for life. For life, Mr. Winger. For life.
jokes.setup: Subway rep presenting special card to Jeff
jokes.punchline: Treats lifetime fast food access as ultimate luxury with dramatic repetition
jokes.explanation: Corporate parody that treats mundane benefit as life-changing. Repetition adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: This card gives you lifetime access to Subway.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: Lifetime. Subway. Access.

dialogue.speaker: Subway Rep
dialogue.line: Do you understand what you're holding?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: A depression?

jokes.text: They're part of the adulthood-begins-at-30 generation.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining why Annie and Abed aren't ready for change
jokes.punchline: Categorizes them in generational cohort that delays maturity
jokes.explanation: Sharp generational observation that perfectly captures Annie and Abed. Economical and insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Annie and Abed aren't ready for change. They're millennials.

jokes.text: Amen. And to think this all started because you wanted to... - Nail you. - Yeah, I regret nothing.
jokes.setup: Britta reflecting on how their relationship began
jokes.punchline: Jeff bluntly confirms his original sexual motivation without shame
jokes.explanation: Great callback to series origins. Jeff's shameless honesty about his motivations is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Do you remember how we first got together? I thought it was something special.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, I wanted to sleep with you.

jokes.text: what did either of us really get done aside from each other?
jokes.setup: Reflecting on their time at Greendale
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'getting each other done' - understanding vs. sexual
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay that works on multiple levels. Britta's casual delivery of innuendo is good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: You know, I think we finally really get each other.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, we do.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I mean, we've been through so much together. We really understand each other now.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Absolutely. I feel like we could get each other done.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: ...Jeff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What? I meant emotionally understand each other!

jokes.text: I should go clean out my locker, she said at the age of 33.
jokes.setup: Britta mentioning normal student activity
jokes.punchline: Self-aware commentary on her age inappropriateness for the activity
jokes.explanation: Perfect self-awareness about the absurdity of adult students doing teen activities. Third-person narration adds to the humor.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I'm going to go to the freshman dorm party tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I know I'm way too old for this, but I'm going anyway because I have poor judgment and boundary issues.

jokes.text: Get a house we can't afford, a dog that makes us angry. Dedicate an entire cabinet to grocery bags... realise we have a favourite brand of olive oil?
jokes.setup: Jeff proposing they get married and settle down
jokes.punchline: Describes domestic life through increasingly specific mundane details
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from big life decisions to absurdly specific domestic details. Jeff's cynical romanticism.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We could get married, settle down. You know, wake up together, have coffee on the porch, watch our kids play in the yard.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We'd argue about whose turn it is to take out the recycling. I'd pretend to fix the dishwasher but actually just jiggle the door handle a few times.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We'd watch TV on a Saturday night and fall asleep before 10 PM. You'd gain thirty pounds, I'd lose my hair, and we'd stop having sex but keep doing it anyway because we'd feel obligated.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We'd take one vacation every other year to a place that's not quite what the brochure promised. We'd have one big fight about money in the car ride home, and that would be our lives.

jokes.text: Medium roughness, high tempo? - Let's make it a number eight.
jokes.setup: Jeff and Britta about to have sex
jokes.punchline: They discuss it like ordering from a technical menu
jokes.explanation: Mechanical approach to intimacy that's both funny and slightly sad. Good character moment about their relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So what are we doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I don't know, what do you want to do?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Well, I'm thinking we could start with some foreplay, maybe work our way up to—

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Can we customize that? I'd like to substitute the foreplay for direct penetration.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Sure. And would you like that with or without eye contact?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Without, please. And can I get the emotional detachment on the side?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Absolutely. Anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Yeah, can we make this to go?

jokes.text: Buried treasure! Lock the doors, close the blinds.
jokes.setup: Annie excited about potential treasure discovery
jokes.punchline: Treats it like a covert operation requiring maximum secrecy
jokes.explanation: Annie's tendency to over-dramatize situations. Callback to Pierce's treasure idea but with characteristic intensity.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, this is a covert operation. We need complete radio silence. No one can know about this treasure.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: I've color-coded the map by risk level. Red zones are off-limits unless we establish a buddy system with hand signals.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: We'll rendezvous at 0600 hours. Wear dark clothing, bring a flashlight with a red filter so we don't compromise our night vision, and for God's sake, nobody tells anyone.

jokes.text: Scunthorpe? I was stationed around Scunthorpe. - No. - Oh, wait, wait.
jokes.setup: Duncan mentions hometown, Hickey recognizes it
jokes.punchline: Duncan's horrified 'No' reaction to potential connection
jokes.explanation: Duncan's panic about potential connection to Hickey. Sets up their shared background reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: I'm from Greendale.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Greendale? I know Greendale. I dated a girl from there in '82.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: O'er the lady's smocks I tarry Through the hollyhocks and glen For a piss and a thrush In Scunthorpe - Then it's off to Henningpen
jokes.setup: Hickey and Duncan discovering shared hometown connection
jokes.punchline: They sing crude local drinking song together
jokes.explanation: Bonding moment through shared vulgar cultural knowledge. Good character development showing their connection.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Wait, you're from Riverside?

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: Born and raised.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: No kidding. My old man used to take me down to the docks there.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: The docks? Ha! I haven't thought about those in years.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Remember that song? The one all the guys used to sing?

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: Oh man, yeah! How does it go?

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Riverside, riverside, where the whiskey flows free...

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: And the ladies are loose and the nights never freeze!

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: That's it! That's it!

dialogue.speaker: Hickey and Duncan
dialogue.line: Riverside, riverside, home of the brave and the bold...

jokes.text: I lost my virginity at Stainsbury's. - My mother was a Stainsbury whore.
jokes.setup: Hickey and Duncan sharing Stainsbury Pub memories
jokes.punchline: Duncan's casual revelation about his mother's profession
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that deepens their connection through shared seedy backgrounds. Duncan's matter-of-fact delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Remember those nights at Stainsbury Pub? Good times.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: The best. My mother used to work there.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Really? What did she do?

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: She was a prostitute.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's... that's quite a revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: Yeah, she was very popular with the regulars.

jokes.text: Mine was missing a thumb. - Oh, my God. - Two thumbs, she got both. - Two thumbs.
jokes.setup: Duncan mentioning his mother's missing thumb
jokes.punchline: Hickey reveals the same woman had both thumbs - it's the same person
jokes.explanation: Perfect reveal that they knew the same person. The thumb detail makes the connection both specific and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: My mother's missing a thumb.

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: What? Your mother had both her thumbs when I knew her.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: You knew my mother?

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Yeah, we dated for a while back in the day.

jokes.text: I mean, you may have slept with my aunt... but given the circumstances, cheers.
jokes.setup: Duncan realizing Hickey knew his prostitute mother
jokes.punchline: Decides to toast the awkward family connection
jokes.explanation: Duncan's philosophical acceptance of bizarre circumstances. Dark humor handled with character-appropriate British politeness.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: Wait, you knew my mother?

dialogue.speaker: Hickey
dialogue.line: Yeah, we go way back.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that makes us family then.

dialogue.speaker: Duncan
dialogue.line: To family connections... however they happen.

tmdb_overview: The Save Greendale Committee's success is short-lived when the board votes for a corporate sellout, but Pelton, Annie and Abed might find a solution.
insight: Meta storytelling structure sustains 82-point episode across 60 jokes in tight 60-minute format.
